text: This is a full movie for a total laparoscopic hysterectomy in simple case and we have the use to do this procedure in ambulatory procedure. The patient leaves the hospital twelve hours after his hospitalization.

The surgeon identifies an ovarian cyst and the left ureter visible below the posterior leaf of the broad ligament. The uterosacral ligament is also visible. A 5mm sealing device is used to begin the procedure by cutting.
The surgeon opens the peritoneum between the left round ligament and the infundibulopelvic (IP) ligament, preparing for further dissection in the laparoscopic hysterectomy procedure.
The surgeon retracts the peritoneum to expose the right dissecting plane, allowing for a wide opening of the retroperitoneal area.

The surgeon continues the dissection laterally towards the descending colon and sigmoid. The peritoneum is retracted cranially, creating a space between the anterior leaf of the broad ligament and the isthmic uterine area, aiming to open the peritoneum safely.

The surgeon continues dissection towards the bladder, with the assistant cranially retracting the proximal part of the peritoneum. The surgeon controls small bleeders as they appear and proceeds with the vesico-vaginal dissection.
The surgeon ensures hemostasis of small bleeders while the uterus is pushed towards the sacrum area using a uterine manipulator.

The surgeon uses a standard uterine manipulator with anterior and posterior valves to open the peritoneum in the correct dissecting plane. The gas aids in visualizing the dissection as the peritoneum is cut towards the right round ligament.
As you see, every time we see a breeder, every time we ensure the hemostasis and with this procedure, you can win time and you can do the entire procedure in less than half an hour.

The surgeon approaches the right infundibulopelvic ligament, continuing lateral dissection towards the ascending colon. The vesicovaginal dissection is performed with the assistant retracting the bladder cranially.

The surgeon cuts the medial aspect of the left bladder pillar, allowing the bladder to be pulled down toward the anterior valve of the uterine manipulator. The cervical vaginal vessel is visible, and hemostasis is ensured to maintain a dry operative field.

The surgeon performs a wide vesico-vaginal dissection by cutting the albin fascia, revealing the anterior aspect of the vaginal valve. The anterior dissection is completed, and hemostasis is ensured before continuing with the hysterectomy.

The surgeon confirms the completion of the vesicovaginal dissection and ensures hemostasis. Attention is then directed to the left infundibulopelvic ligament, where the peritoneum surrounding the vessel is carefully removed to allow direct application of sealing energy on the vessel, following the Clermont-Ferrand technique.
The surgeon opens the posterior leaf of the broad ligament, increasing the distance between the ureter and the infundibulopelvic ligament, ensuring the ureter remains far from the operative field.

The surgeon tracts cranially the adnexal structures and opens the posterior leaf of the broad ligament toward the isthmic uterine area. The peritoneum is cut on the opposite side, revealing the ureter positioned far from the operative field.
The surgeon applies sealing energy to the freed vessels, ensuring hemostasis by sealing the tissue over a two-centimeter length.

The surgeon allows the energy device to perform hemostasis before safely cutting the ligament. The fatty tissue is secured, and the procedure progresses towards the upper part of the left utero-sacral ligament. The peritoneum surrounding the left uterine artery is opened, taking care to avoid damaging the uterine vein, which enhances uterine mobility.

The surgeon skeletonizes the left uterine pedicle, exposing the vessel for sealing. The uterus is pulled to the opposite side, and sealing energy is applied at the ismic area without cutting the vessel to reduce backflow. The vessel is controlled over a three-centimeter length.

The surgeon ensures hemostasis to decrease upper backflow and controls the uterine vein hemostasis without cutting the pedicle. The procedure is mirrored on the opposite side, preparing to control the opposite uterine artery.

The surgeon retracts the adnexa cranially, exposing the peritoneum surrounding the infundibulopelvic ligament. The assistants demonstrate the peritoneum, which is then cut, similar to the opposite side. The ureter is visibly distant from the operative field, ensuring a safe procedure.

The surgeon cuts the peritoneum toward the upper part of the right utero-sacral ligament, ensuring hemostasis. A window is opened, and sealing energy is applied on the IP ligament, maintaining a safe distance from the uterus.

The surgeon applies sealing energy to ensure hemostasis before safely cutting the pedicle. The connective tissue in the retroperitoneal space is then opened, demonstrating effective hemostasis without the use of bipolar energy.

The surgeon cuts the peritoneum along the posterior leaf of the broad ligament, extending toward the upper part of the right uterosacral ligament. This dissection increases uterine mobility, with visible cutting near the posterior valve.

The surgeon controls the right uterine pedicle by removing the peritoneum and applying sealing energy, similar to the opposite side. The uterus is pulled to the left, increasing the distance between the uterine artery and the ureter, as the dissection progresses from the ismic area downward by two centimeters.
The surgeon seals the vessel at the ismic area, allowing for safe control and cutting of the pedicle. The posterior vena is controlled, and peritoneal hemostasis is ensured.
The surgeon cuts the pedicle and continues dissection in the intrafacial plane toward the bladder, ensuring hemostasis throughout the procedure.

The surgeon ensures backflow and performs the same procedure on the opposite side, maintaining a bloodless field by reapplying sealing energy. The uterus is pulled to the right, and the entire pedicle is tracked with grasping forceps for better contact with the vessel and ligature system, controlling the pedicle on both sides.

The surgeon uses a monopolar needle to cut the vagina on the uterine manipulator, focusing on the left lateral aspect and the upper part of the uterosacral ligament. Hemostasis is ensured, addressing a small bleeder with coagulating current.

The surgeon cuts the right lateral aspect of the vagina using a section current, while smoke is released from the operative site. The valve of the operative trocar is opened to remove the smoke generated during the procedure.

The surgeon uses a monopolar hook to cut the posterior aspect of the vagina, ensuring hemostasis. The upper part of the right uterosacral ligament is also cut. The hook is then placed on the left operative trocar to be perpendicular to the posterior aspect of the vagina, allowing for precise circumscription under visual control.
The assistant aids in visualizing the section line as the surgeon replaces the monopolar needle in the medial trocar to cut the residual tissue. The uterus is then extracted vaginally.

The assistant, positioned between the patient's legs, uses the uterine manipulator to pull the uterus through the vagina. The surgeon removes the small uterus transvaginally without needing additional hemostasis with the bipolar device.

The surgeon uses bipolar energy and places stitches with a Vicryl zero fourteen millimeter needle. The vagina is rinsed to check for any significant bleeding, which is not present. Suturing is performed rapidly, with careful attention to the location of the vaginal mucosa and bladder.

The surgeon begins suturing from the lateral aspect of the vagina, passing the needle through the upper part of the uterosacral ligament to reduce the risk of postoperative vaginal prolapse. A rudder knot technique is used for closing the vagina, with the knot locked directly on the left aspect of the vagina. An additional knot is tied to ensure the quality of the closure, and the same procedure is performed on the opposite side.

The surgeon passes the knot through the 12mm trocar located in the left iliac fossa. The assistants rinse the pelvis to remove clots, ensuring a clear view. The uterine artery is visibly sealed, and the needle is inserted into the vaginal mucosa.

The surgeon places a stitch in the upper part of the uterosacral ligament and inserts the needle into the peritoneum. A rubber knot is used to secure the closure, followed by cutting the excess suture and adding an extra stitch for reinforcement. Both angles of the vagina are now closed.

The surgeon closes the medial part of the vagina using a single stitch for the total laparoscopic hysterectomy. The procedure begins with a rudder knot for the first three knots, followed by intracorporeal knotting using the remaining stitch.

And in our countries, if you use only one knot and you do the procedure in half an hour and the patient can leave the clinic the same day, you can do an economical way of surgical procedure as you see we do the third rotor knot as usual we close this knot

The surgeon performs intracorporeal knotting to close the vaginal cuff, demonstrating the technique in real-time without cutting. The procedure appears safe and fast, with visible assistance from trained staff.

The surgeon performs an intracorporeal knotting technique using the same stitch from the beginning of the procedure. The final stitch is an X knot, ensuring the vaginal mucosa is incorporated within the stitch to securely close the vagina.

The assistants point out the vaginal mucosa, and the surgeon places the final stitch inside the mucosa to minimize the risk of post-operative vaginal wall bleeding. The procedure is completed using a classical technique for ambulatory laparoscopic total hysterectomy.

The surgeon completes the laparoscopic hysterectomy procedure, ensuring hemostasis by applying small bipolar current to control bleeding on the left part of the vagina and the upper part of the uterosacral ligament. The small bleeders are managed with precise application of bipolar energy.
